title: Harvard University

infobox.value: * [AAU](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") * [COFHE](/wiki/Consortium_on_Financing_Higher_Education "Consortium on Financing Higher Education") * [NAICU](/wiki/National_Association_of_Independent_Colleges_and_Universities "National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities") * [UArctic](/wiki/University_of_the_Arctic "University of the Arctic") * [URA](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") * [Space-grant](/wiki/National_Space_Grant_College_and_Fellowship_Program "National Space Grant College and Fellowship Program")
~2,400 faculty members (and >10,400 academic appointments in affiliated teaching hospitals)[[8]](#cite_note-glance-8)

* [NCAA Division I](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I "NCAA Division I") [FCS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Championship_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Championship Subdivision") – [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") * [ECAC Hockey](/wiki/ECAC_Hockey "ECAC Hockey") * [NEISA](/wiki/New_England_Intercollegiate_Sailing_Association "New England Intercollegiate Sailing Association") * [CWPA](/wiki/Collegiate_Water_Polo_Association "Collegiate Water Polo Association") * [IRA](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Rowing_Association "Intercollegiate Rowing Association") * [EAWRC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Women%27s_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Women's Rowing Colleges") * [EARC](/wiki/Eastern_Association_of_Rowing_Colleges "Eastern Association of Rowing Colleges") * [EISA](/wiki/Eastern_Intercollegiate_Ski_Association "Eastern Intercollegiate Ski Association")

is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"), United States. Founded in 1636, and named in 1639 for its first benefactor, the [Puritan](/wiki/History_of_the_Puritans_in_North_America "History of the Puritans in North America") clergyman [John Harvard](/wiki/John_Harvard_(clergyman) "John Harvard (clergyman)"), it is the oldest institution of higher learning in the United States. Its influence, wealth, and rankings have made it one of the most prestigious universities in the world.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

**Harvard University** is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"), United States. Founded in 1636, and named in 1639 for its first benefactor, the [Puritan](/wiki/History_of_the_Puritans_in_North_America "History of the Puritans in North America") clergyman [John Harvard](/wiki/John_Harvard_(clergyman) "John Harvard (clergyman)"), it is the oldest institution of higher learning in the United States. Its influence, wealth, and rankings have made it one of the most prestigious universities in the world.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Harvard was founded and authorized by the [Massachusetts General Court](/wiki/Massachusetts_General_Court#Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts General Court"), the governing legislature of [colonial](/wiki/Colonial_history_of_the_United_States "Colonial history of the United States")-era [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony").[[13]](#cite_note-13) While never formally affiliated with any [Protestant denomination](/wiki/Protestant_church "Protestant church"), Harvard trained [Congregational](/wiki/Congregationalism_in_the_United_States "Congregationalism in the United States") clergy until its curriculum and student body were gradually secularized in the 18th century. By the 19th century, Harvard had emerged as the most prominent academic and cultural institution among the [Boston elite](/wiki/Boston_Brahmin "Boston Brahmin").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Following the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), under [Harvard president](/wiki/President_of_Harvard_University "President of Harvard University") [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot")'s long tenure from 1869 to 1909, Harvard developed multiple professional schools, which transformed it into a modern research university. In 1900, Harvard co-founded the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").[[16]](#cite_note-AAU-16) [James B. Conant](/wiki/James_B._Conant "James B. Conant") led the university through the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and World War II, and liberalized admissions after the war.

The university has ten academic faculties and a faculty attached to [Harvard Radcliffe Institute](/wiki/Harvard_Radcliffe_Institute "Harvard Radcliffe Institute"). The [Faculty of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences") offers study in a wide range of undergraduate and graduate [academic disciplines](/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline"), and other faculties offer graduate degrees, including professional degrees. Harvard has three campuses:[[17]](#cite_note-17)
the main campus, a 209-acre (85 ha) in Cambridge centered on [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard"); an adjoining campus immediately across [Charles River](/wiki/Charles_River "Charles River") in the [Allston](/wiki/Allston "Allston") neighborhood of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"); and the medical campus in Boston's [Longwood Medical Area](/wiki/Longwood_Medical_and_Academic_Area "Longwood Medical and Academic Area").[[18]](#cite_note-Campus-18) [Harvard's endowment](/wiki/Harvard_University_endowment "Harvard University endowment"), valued at $55.7 billion, makes it the [wealthiest academic institution](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_the_United_States_by_endowment "List of colleges and universities in the United States by endowment") in the world.[[19]](#cite_note-BGendow-19)[[20]](#cite_note-HFRendow-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) [Harvard Library](/wiki/Harvard_Library "Harvard Library"), with more than 20 million volumes, is the world's largest [academic library](/wiki/Academic_library "Academic library").

Harvard alumni, faculty, and researchers include [188 living billionaires](/wiki/List_of_universities_by_number_of_billionaire_alumni "List of universities by number of billionaire alumni"), [8 U.S. presidents](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_by_education "List of presidents of the United States by education"), [24 heads of state and 31 heads of government](/wiki/List_of_Harvard_University_politicians "List of Harvard University politicians"), founders of notable companies, [Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_university_affiliation "List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation"), [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"), [members of Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), [MacArthur Fellows](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program"), [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship"), [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"), [Turing Award Recipients](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award"), [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") recipients, and [Fulbright Scholars](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program"); by most metrics, Harvard University ranks among the top universities in the world in each of these categories.[[Notes 1]](#cite_note-laureates-22) Harvard students and alumni have also collectively won 10 [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") and [110 Olympic medals](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medalist_students_and_alumni "List of American universities with Olympic medalist students and alumni"), including 46 gold medals.

## History

Main article: [History of Harvard University](/wiki/History_of_Harvard_University "History of Harvard University")

### Colonial era

See also: [John Harvard (clergyman)](/wiki/John_Harvard_(clergyman) "John Harvard (clergyman)"), [Nathaniel Eaton](/wiki/Nathaniel_Eaton "Nathaniel Eaton"), and [Increase Mather](/wiki/Increase_Mather "Increase Mather")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/A_Westerly_View_of_the_Colledges_in_Cambridge_New_England_by_Paul_Revere.jpeg/250px-A_Westerly_View_of_the_Colledges_in_Cambridge_New_England_by_Paul_Revere.jpeg)](/wiki/File:A_Westerly_View_of_the_Colledges_in_Cambridge_New_England_by_Paul_Revere.jpeg)

A 1767 engraving of [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College") by [Paul Revere](/wiki/Paul_Revere "Paul Revere")

Harvard was founded in 1636 by a vote of the [Great and General Court](/wiki/Massachusetts_General_Court "Massachusetts General Court") of the [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony"). Its first headmaster, [Nathaniel Eaton](/wiki/Nathaniel_Eaton "Nathaniel Eaton"), took office the following year. In 1638, the university acquired [English North America](/wiki/British_North_America "British North America")'s first known [printing press](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press").[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) The same year, on his deathbed, [John Harvard](/wiki/John_Harvard_(clergyman) "John Harvard (clergyman)"), a [Puritan](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") clergyman who had emigrated to the colony from England, bequeathed the emerging college £780 and his library of some 320 volumes;[[24]](#cite_note-25) the following year, it was named [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College").

In 1643, a Harvard publication defined the college's purpose: "[to] advance learning and perpetuate it to posterity, dreading to leave an illiterate ministry to the churches when our present ministers shall lie in the dust."[[25]](#cite_note-26)

In its early years, the college trained many Puritan [Congregational](/wiki/Established_Congregational_churches "Established Congregational churches") ministers[[26]](#cite_note-27) and offered a [classical curriculum](/wiki/Classical_education "Classical education") based on the English university model exemplified by the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"), where many colonial Massachusetts leaders had studied prior to emigrating to the colony. Harvard College never formally affiliated with any particular [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") denomination, but its curriculum conformed to the tenets of Puritanism.[[27]](#cite_note-28) In 1650, the charter for [Harvard Corporation](/wiki/President_and_Fellows_of_Harvard_College "President and Fellows of Harvard College"), the college's governing body, was granted.

From 1681 to 1701, [Increase Mather](/wiki/Increase_Mather "Increase Mather"), a Puritan clergyman, served as Harvard's sixth [president](/wiki/President_of_Harvard_University "President of Harvard University"). In 1708, [John Leverett](/wiki/John_Leverett_the_Younger "John Leverett the Younger") became Harvard's seventh president and the first president who was not also a clergyman.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Harvard faculty and students largely supported the [Patriot](/wiki/Patriot_(American_Revolution) "Patriot (American Revolution)") cause during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution").[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)

The earliest known official seal of Harvard University, commonly referred to as the Seal of 1650 or the In Christi Gloriam seal, features a square shield bearing three open books arranged around a central chevron. This design symbolizes the pursuit of learning under divine guidance. The motto IN CHRISTI GLORIAM ("To the glory of Christ") appears prominently on the seal, which is encircled by the Latin inscription SIGILL COL HARVARD CANTAB NOV ANGL 1650, meaning "Seal of Harvard College, Cambridge, New England, 1650." This seal reflects the original religious mission of the institution.

In 1885, the Harvard Corporation adopted a revised design known as the Appleton Seal, based on an earlier version created by President Josiah Quincy in 1843. Designed by William Sumner Appleton (Harvard AB 1860), the seal features a triangular shield bearing three open books with the motto VERITAS ("Truth"). Surrounding the shield is the motto CHRISTO ET ECCLESIÆ ("For Christ and the Church"), and the outer border bears the inscription SIGILLVM ACADEMIÆ HARVARDINÆ IN NOV. ANG. ("Seal of Harvard College in New England"). This version of the seal sought to harmonize the university's intellectual pursuits with its ecclesiastical roots.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

### 19th century

See also: [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot") and [Samuel Webber](/wiki/Samuel_Webber "Samuel Webber")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/John_Harvard_statue.jpg/250px-John_Harvard_statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Harvard_statue.jpg)

The [John Harvard statue](/wiki/John_Harvard_statue "John Harvard statue") in [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard")

In the 19th century, Harvard was influenced by [Enlightenment Age](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") ideas, including reason and free will, which were widespread among [Congregational](/wiki/Congregationalism_in_the_United_States "Congregationalism in the United States") ministers and which placed these ministers and their congregations at odds with more traditionalist, [Calvinist](/wiki/Reformed_Christianity "Reformed Christianity") pastors and clergies.[[32]](#cite_note-Dorrien-33): 1–4 Following the death of [Hollis Professor of Divinity](/wiki/Hollis_Professor_of_Divinity "Hollis Professor of Divinity") [David Tappan](/wiki/David_Tappan "David Tappan") in 1803 and that of [Joseph Willard](/wiki/Joseph_Willard "Joseph Willard"), Harvard's eleventh president, the following year, a struggle broke out over their replacements. In 1805, [Henry Ware](/wiki/Henry_Ware_(Unitarian) "Henry Ware (Unitarian)") was elected to replace Tappan as Hollis chair. Two years later, in 1807, liberal [Samuel Webber](/wiki/Samuel_Webber "Samuel Webber") was appointed as Harvard's 13th president, representing a shift from traditional ideas at Harvard to more liberal and [Arminian](/wiki/Arminianism "Arminianism") ideas.[[32]](#cite_note-Dorrien-33): 4–5[[33]](#cite_note-34): 24

In 1816, Harvard University launched new language programs in the study of [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") and [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and appointed [George Ticknor](/wiki/George_Ticknor "George Ticknor") the university's first professor for these language programs.

From 1869 to 1909, [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot"), Harvard University's 21st president, decreased the historically favored position of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") in the curriculum, opening it to student self-direction. Though Eliot was an influential figure in the secularization of U.S. higher education, he was motivated primarily by [Transcendentalist](/wiki/Transcendentalism "Transcendentalism") and [Unitarian](/wiki/Unitarianism "Unitarianism") convictions influenced by [William Ellery Channing](/wiki/William_Ellery_Channing "William Ellery Channing"), [Ralph Waldo Emerson](/wiki/Ralph_Waldo_Emerson "Ralph Waldo Emerson"), and others, rather than secularism. In the late 19th century, Harvard University's graduate schools began admitting women in small numbers.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### 20th century

See also: [A. Lawrence Lowell](/wiki/A._Lawrence_Lowell "A. Lawrence Lowell") and [James B. Conant](/wiki/James_B._Conant "James B. Conant")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Rummell%2C_Richard_Harvard_University.jpg/250px-Rummell%2C_Richard_Harvard_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rummell,_Richard_Harvard_University.jpg)

A 1906 aerial watercolor portrait of Harvard University[[35]](#cite_note-36)

In 1900, Harvard became a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities").[[16]](#cite_note-AAU-16) For the first few decades of the 20th century, the Harvard student body was predominantly "old-stock, high-status [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), especially [Episcopalians](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)"), [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregational_church "Congregational church"), and [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism")," according to sociologist and author [Jerome Karabel](/wiki/Jerome_Karabel "Jerome Karabel").[[36]](#cite_note-37)

Over the 20th century, as its endowment burgeoned and prominent intellectuals and professors affiliated with it, Harvard University's reputation as one of the world's most prestigious universities grew notably. The university's enrollment also underwent substantial growth, a product of both the founding of new graduate academic programs and an expansion of the [undergraduate college](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College"). [Radcliffe College](/wiki/Radcliffe_College "Radcliffe College") emerged as the female counterpart of Harvard College, becoming one of the most prominent schools in the nation for women.

In 1923, a year after the proportion of [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") students at Harvard reached 20%, [A. Lawrence Lowell](/wiki/A._Lawrence_Lowell "A. Lawrence Lowell"), the university's 22nd president, unsuccessfully proposed capping the admission of Jewish students to 15% of the undergraduate population. Lowell also refused to mandate forced desegregation in the university's [freshman dormitories](/wiki/List_of_Harvard_College_freshman_dormitories "List of Harvard College freshman dormitories"), writing that, "We owe to the colored man the same opportunities for education that we do to the white man, but we do not owe to him to force him and the white into social relations that are not, or may not be, mutually congenial."[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Between 1933 and 1953, Harvard University was led by [James B. Conant](/wiki/James_B._Conant "James B. Conant"), the university's 23rd president, who reinvigorated the university's creative scholarship in an effort to guarantee Harvard's preeminence among the nation and world's emerging research institutions. Conant viewed higher education as a vehicle of opportunity for the talented rather than an entitlement for the wealthy, and devised programs to identify, recruit, and support talented youth. In 1945, under Conant's leadership, an influential 268-page report, *[General Education in a Free Society](/wiki/General_Education_in_a_Free_Society "General Education in a Free Society")*, was published by Harvard faculty, which remains one of the most important works in [curriculum studies](/wiki/Curriculum_studies "Curriculum studies"),[[41]](#cite_note-42) and women were first admitted to the [medical school](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School").[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Between 1945 and 1960, admissions were standardized to open the university to a more diverse group of students. Following the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), for example, special exams were developed so veterans could be considered for admission.[[43]](#cite_note-44) No longer drawing mostly from prestigious [prep schools](/wiki/College-preparatory_school "College-preparatory school") in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), the undergraduate college became accessible to striving middle class students from public schools; many more Jews and Catholics were admitted, but Blacks, Hispanics, and Asians remained underrepresented.[[44]](#cite_note-45) Over the second half of the 20th century, however, the university became incrementally more diverse.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Between 1971 and 1999, Harvard controlled undergraduate admission, instruction, and housing for Radcliffe's women; in 1999, Radcliffe was formally merged into Harvard University.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

### 21st century

See also: [Drew Gilpin Faust](/wiki/Drew_Gilpin_Faust "Drew Gilpin Faust"), [Lawrence Bacow](/wiki/Lawrence_Bacow "Lawrence Bacow"), [Claudine Gay](/wiki/Claudine_Gay "Claudine Gay"), and [Alan Garber](/wiki/Alan_Garber "Alan Garber")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Harvard_Yard_at_Night_03.jpg/250px-Harvard_Yard_at_Night_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvard_Yard_at_Night_03.jpg)

An aerial view of Harvard University at night in 2017

On July 1, 2007, [Drew Gilpin Faust](/wiki/Drew_Gilpin_Faust "Drew Gilpin Faust"), dean of [Harvard Radcliffe Institute](/wiki/Harvard_Radcliffe_Institute "Harvard Radcliffe Institute"), was appointed Harvard's 28th and the university's first female president.[[47]](#cite_note-48) On July 1, 2018, Faust retired and joined the board of [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), and [Lawrence Bacow](/wiki/Lawrence_Bacow "Lawrence Bacow") became Harvard's [29th president](/wiki/President_of_Harvard_University "President of Harvard University").[[48]](#cite_note-49)

In February 2023, approximately 6,000 Harvard workers attempted to organize a union.[[49]](#cite_note-50)

Bacow retired in June 2023, and on July 1 [Claudine Gay](/wiki/Claudine_Gay "Claudine Gay"), a Harvard professor in the Government and African American Studies departments and dean of the [Faculty of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences"), became Harvard's 30th president. In January 2024, just six months into her presidency, Gay resigned following [allegations of antisemitism](/wiki/Claudine_Gay#Congressional_hearing_on_antisemitism "Claudine Gay") and [plagiarism](/wiki/Claudine_Gay#Plagiarism_investigations "Claudine Gay").[[50]](#cite_note-51) Gay was succeeded by [Alan Garber](/wiki/Alan_Garber "Alan Garber"), the university's provost, who was appointed interim president. In August 2024, the university announced that Garber would be appointed Harvard's 31st president through the end of the 2026–27 academic year.

#### Second presidency of Donald Trump

See also: [Education policy of the second Trump administration § Actions against universities](/wiki/Education_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration#Actions_against_universities "Education policy of the second Trump administration")

In February 2025, [Leo Terrell](/wiki/Leo_Terrell "Leo Terrell"), the head of the Trump administration's [Task Force to Combat Antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_United_States#Efforts_to_combat_antisemitism "Antisemitism in the United States"), announced that he would investigate Harvard University as part of the Department of Justice's broader investigation into [antisemitism on college campuses](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_US_higher_education "Antisemitism in US higher education").[[51]](#cite_note-52)

In April 2025, the United States federal government under President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") threatened to withhold nearly $9 billion in government funds from the university unless the university complied with government demands to modify many of its policies. This threat was part of a broader battle over universities' autonomy following contentious [student protests](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses") against the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), and followed [similar demands](/wiki/Education_policy_of_the_second_Donald_Trump_administration#Actions_against_universities "Education policy of the second Donald Trump administration") made of [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University").[[52]](#cite_note-:0-53) The university's leadership resisted the government's demands, claiming that they were an unlawful overreach of government authority.[[53]](#cite_note-54) In response, the [US Department of Education](/wiki/US_Department_of_Education "US Department of Education") announced they were freezing $2.3 billion in federal funds to Harvard.[[54]](#cite_note-guard-14apr2025-55) The [Department of Homeland Security](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Homeland_Security "United States Department of Homeland Security") subsequently threatened to revoke Harvard's eligibility to host [international students](/wiki/International_student "International student").[[52]](#cite_note-:0-53) Harvard responded by filing [a lawsuit](/wiki/President_and_Fellows_of_Harvard_College_v._Department_of_Health_and_Human_Services "President and Fellows of Harvard College v. Department of Health and Human Services") against the Trump administration in the [District Court of Massachusetts](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_District_of_Massachusetts "United States District Court for the District of Massachusetts"), arguing that the freezing of funds was unconstitutional.[[55]](#cite_note-guard-21apr2025-56)[[56]](#cite_note-nbc-21apr2025-57)[[57]](#cite_note-guard-21jul2025-58)

In May 2025, education secretary [Linda McMahon](/wiki/Linda_McMahon "Linda McMahon") informed Harvard president Garber that the federal government would no longer provide grant funding until the university complied with the Trump administration's demands.[[58]](#cite_note-guard-5may2025-59) The following week, the Trump administration cut an additional $450 million in grants to the school.[[59]](#cite_note-ap-13may2025-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Exhibit_25._May_22%2C_2025_DHS_Decertification_Letter.pdf/page1-250px-Exhibit_25._May_22%2C_2025_DHS_Decertification_Letter.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exhibit_25._May_22,_2025_DHS_Decertification_Letter.pdf)

Decertification Letter sent by Kristi Noem on May 22, 2025

Later that same month, Department of Homeland Security secretary [Kristi Noem](/wiki/Kristi_Noem "Kristi Noem") announced that Harvard's [Student and Exchange Visitor Program](/wiki/Student_and_Exchange_Visitor_Program "Student and Exchange Visitor Program") certification had been revoked, barring Harvard from hosting international students.[[60]](#cite_note-guard-22may2025-61)[[61]](#cite_note-dhs-22may2025-62) The following day, Harvard sued the Trump administration for banning them from enrolling international students and U.S. District Judge [Allison Burroughs](/wiki/Allison_D._Burroughs "Allison D. Burroughs") granted a temporary restraining order stopping the ban.[[62]](#cite_note-guard-23may2025-1-63)[[63]](#cite_note-guard-23may2025-2-64)[[64]](#cite_note-harvard-suit-23may2025-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) On June 16, 2025, Burroughs postponed a ruling after hearing arguments from lawyers on both sides, leaving the temporary block in place for another week.[[66]](#cite_note-npr-16jun2025-67)

On May 30, 2025, the [State Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State") ordered all US embassies and consulates to conduct "comprehensive and thorough vetting" of the online presence of anyone seeking to visit Harvard from abroad.[[67]](#cite_note-guard-30may2025-68)

On June 4, 2025, Trump issued a proclamation restricting international students from studying at Harvard, and directing the State Department to consider revoking the visas of current international students studying at that university.[[68]](#cite_note-guard-5jun2025-69)[[69]](#cite_note-wh-proc-4jun2025-70) The following day, Harvard filed a legal challenge, amending their existing federal complaint against the administration.[[70]](#cite_note-nbc-5jun2025-71)[[71]](#cite_note-guard2-5jun2025-72)[[72]](#cite_note-cl-5jun2025-73)

On June 20, Harvard was granted an injunction allowing it to continue hosting international students as litigation continues.[[73]](#cite_note-74) On June 30, a Trump administration investigation found Harvard violated federal civil rights law by failing to protect Jewish students, faculty, and staff.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

On September 3, 2025 US District Judge [Allison Burroughs](/wiki/Allison_D._Burroughs "Allison D. Burroughs") ruled the Trump administration illegally froze more than $2 billion in research funding stating the administration "...violated Harvard's free-speech rights as well as the US Civil Rights Act."[[75]](#cite_note-76)

In February 2026, Trump announced that his administration would seek $1 billion in damages from Harvard. In a post on [Truth Social](/wiki/Truth_Social "Truth Social"), Trump accused Harvard of supplying misleading information to *The New York Times*. Administration officials claimed that the university failed to adequately address antisemitism during pro-Palestinian protests, a claim Harvard has denied.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

On February 6th, Secretary of Defense [Pete Hegseth](/wiki/Pete_Hegseth "Pete Hegseth") announced that the [Pentagon](/wiki/The_Pentagon "The Pentagon") would start to sever ties to Harvard with its graduate-level military training courses. Hegseth accused Harvard of becoming a "factory for woke ideology and a breeding ground for anti-American radicals..." as a reason for discontinuing the programs.[[77]](#cite_note-78) A week later, Harvard along with other prestigious American Universities was listed as “Moderate to High Risk” in a Department of Defense memo for Assisted Military Tuition, and could be barred for service members for tuition assistance.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

## Campuses

### Cambridge

See also: [Harvard Divinity School](/wiki/Harvard_Divinity_School "Harvard Divinity School"), [Harvard Graduate School of Design](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Design "Harvard Graduate School of Design"), [Harvard Graduate School of Education](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Education "Harvard Graduate School of Education"), [Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_John_A._Paulson_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Sciences "Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences"), [Harvard Kennedy School](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School"), [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School"), and [Harvard Radcliffe Institute](/wiki/Harvard_Radcliffe_Institute "Harvard Radcliffe Institute")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/Massachusetts_Hall%2C_Harvard_University.JPG/250px-Massachusetts_Hall%2C_Harvard_University.JPG)](/wiki/File:Massachusetts_Hall,_Harvard_University.JPG)

[Massachusetts Hall](/wiki/Massachusetts_Hall_(Harvard_University) "Massachusetts Hall (Harvard University)"), Harvard's oldest building, constructed in 1720[[79]](#cite_note-80)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Sanders_theater_2009y.JPG/250px-Sanders_theater_2009y.JPG)](/wiki/File:Sanders_theater_2009y.JPG)

[Memorial Hall](/wiki/Memorial_Hall_(Harvard_University) "Memorial Hall (Harvard University)"), built on the main Cambridge campus in 1870

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Harvard_memorial_church_winter_2009.JPG/250px-Harvard_memorial_church_winter_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Harvard_memorial_church_winter_2009.JPG)

[Memorial Church](/wiki/Memorial_Church_of_Harvard_University "Memorial Church of Harvard University"), dedicated and opened in 1932 on [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/HarvardYard.jpg/250px-HarvardYard.jpg)](/wiki/File:HarvardYard.jpg)

[Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard") at the center of Harvard's main campus in [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts")

The 209-acre (85 ha) main campus of Harvard University is centered on [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard"), colloquially known as "the Yard", in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"), about three miles (five km) west-northwest of downtown [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), and extending to the surrounding [Harvard Square](/wiki/Harvard_Square "Harvard Square") neighborhood. The Yard houses several Harvard buildings, including four of the university's libraries, [Houghton](/wiki/Houghton_Library "Houghton Library"), [Lamont](/wiki/Lamont_Library "Lamont Library"), [Pusey](/wiki/Pusey_Library "Pusey Library"), and [Widener](/wiki/Widener_Library "Widener Library"). Also on Harvard Yard are [Massachusetts Hall](/wiki/Massachusetts_Hall_(Harvard_University) "Massachusetts Hall (Harvard University)"), built between 1718 and 1720 and the university's oldest still standing building, [Memorial Church](/wiki/Memorial_Church_of_Harvard_University "Memorial Church of Harvard University"), and [University Hall](/wiki/University_Hall_(Harvard_University) "University Hall (Harvard University)").

Harvard Yard and adjacent areas include the main academic buildings of the [Faculty of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences"), including [Sever Hall](/wiki/Sever_Hall "Sever Hall"), [Harvard Hall](/wiki/Harvard_Hall "Harvard Hall"), and [freshman dormitories](/wiki/List_of_Harvard_College_freshman_dormitories "List of Harvard College freshman dormitories"). Upperclassmen live in the twelve [residential houses](/wiki/Harvard_House_system "Harvard House system"): nine south of Harvard Yard near the [Charles River](/wiki/Charles_River "Charles River"), and three on the [Radcliffe Quadrangle](/wiki/Radcliffe_Quadrangle_(Harvard) "Radcliffe Quadrangle (Harvard)"), which formerly housed [Radcliffe College](/wiki/Radcliffe_College "Radcliffe College") students. Each house is a community of undergraduates, faculty deans, and resident tutors, with its own dining hall, library, and recreational facilities.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

Also on the main campus in Cambridge are the [Law](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School"), [Divinity](/wiki/Harvard_Divinity_School "Harvard Divinity School") (theology), [Engineering and Applied Science](/wiki/Harvard_John_A._Paulson_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Sciences "Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences"), [Design](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Design "Harvard Graduate School of Design") (architecture), [Education](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Education "Harvard Graduate School of Education"), [Kennedy](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School") (public policy), and [Extension](/wiki/Harvard_Extension_School "Harvard Extension School") schools, and [Harvard Radcliffe Institute](/wiki/Harvard_Radcliffe_Institute "Harvard Radcliffe Institute") in Radcliffe Yard.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Harvard also has commercial real estate holdings in Cambridge.[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84)

### Allston

Main article: [Harvard University's expansion in Allston, Massachusetts](/wiki/Harvard_University%27s_expansion_in_Allston,_Massachusetts "Harvard University's expansion in Allston, Massachusetts")

[Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School"), [Harvard Innovation Labs](/wiki/Harvard_Innovation_Labs "Harvard Innovation Labs"), and many athletics facilities, including [Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium"), are located on a 358-acre (145 ha) campus in the [Allston](/wiki/Allston "Allston") section of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") across the [John W. Weeks Bridge](/wiki/John_W._Weeks_Bridge "John W. Weeks Bridge"), which crosses the [Charles River](/wiki/Charles_River "Charles River") and connects the Allston and Cambridge campuses.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

The university is actively expanding into Allston, where it now owns more land than in Cambridge.[[85]](#cite_note-86) Plans include new construction and renovation for the Business School, a hotel and conference center, graduate student housing, Harvard Stadium, and other athletics facilities.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

In 2021, the [Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_John_A._Paulson_School_of_Engineering_and_Applied_Sciences "Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences") expanded into the new Allston-based Science and Engineering Complex (SEC), which is more than 500,000 square feet in size.[[87]](#cite_note-88) SEC is adjacent to the Enterprise Research Campus, the Business School, and Harvard Innovation Labs, and designed to encourage technology- and life science-focused startups and collaborations with mature companies.[[88]](#cite_note-89)

### Longwood

Main article: [Longwood Medical and Academic Area](/wiki/Longwood_Medical_and_Academic_Area "Longwood Medical and Academic Area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Harvard_Medical_School_HDR.jpg/250px-Harvard_Medical_School_HDR.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvard_Medical_School_HDR.jpg)

[Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School") in the [Longwood Medical and Academic Area](/wiki/Longwood_Medical_and_Academic_Area "Longwood Medical and Academic Area") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston")

The university's schools of [Medicine](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School"), [Dental Medicine](/wiki/Harvard_School_of_Dental_Medicine "Harvard School of Dental Medicine"), and [Public Health](/wiki/Harvard_T.H._Chan_School_of_Public_Health "Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health") are located on a 21-acre (8.5 ha) campus in the [Longwood Medical and Academic Area](/wiki/Longwood_Medical_and_Academic_Area "Longwood Medical and Academic Area") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), about 3.3 miles (5.3 km) south of the Cambridge campus.[[18]](#cite_note-Campus-18)

Several Harvard-affiliated hospitals and research institutes are also in Longwood, including [Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center](/wiki/Beth_Israel_Deaconess_Medical_Center "Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center"), [Boston Children's Hospital](/wiki/Boston_Children%27s_Hospital "Boston Children's Hospital"), [Brigham and Women's Hospital](/wiki/Brigham_and_Women%27s_Hospital "Brigham and Women's Hospital"), [Dana–Farber Cancer Institute](/wiki/Dana%E2%80%93Farber_Cancer_Institute "Dana–Farber Cancer Institute"), [Joslin Diabetes Center](/wiki/Joslin_Diabetes_Center "Joslin Diabetes Center"), and the [Wyss Institute for Biologically Inspired Engineering](/wiki/Wyss_Institute_for_Biologically_Inspired_Engineering "Wyss Institute for Biologically Inspired Engineering"). Additional affiliates, including [Massachusetts General Hospital](/wiki/Massachusetts_General_Hospital "Massachusetts General Hospital"), are located throughout [Greater Boston](/wiki/Greater_Boston "Greater Boston").

### Other

Harvard owns [Dumbarton Oaks](/wiki/Dumbarton_Oaks "Dumbarton Oaks"), a research library in Washington, D.C., [Harvard Forest](/wiki/Harvard_Forest "Harvard Forest") in [Petersham, Massachusetts](/wiki/Petersham,_Massachusetts "Petersham, Massachusetts"), Concord Field Station in [Estabrook Woods](/wiki/Estabrook_Woods "Estabrook Woods") in [Concord, Massachusetts](/wiki/Concord,_Massachusetts "Concord, Massachusetts"),[[89]](#cite_note-90)
the [Villa I Tatti](/wiki/Villa_I_Tatti "Villa I Tatti") research center in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Italy,[[90]](#cite_note-91) and the Center for Hellenic Studies in Greece. The Harvard Shanghai Center in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), China,[[91]](#cite_note-92)
and [Arnold Arboretum](/wiki/Arnold_Arboretum "Arnold Arboretum") in the [Jamaica Plain](/wiki/Jamaica_Plain "Jamaica Plain") neighborhood of Boston.

## Organization and administration

### Governance

See also: [Harvard Board of Overseers](/wiki/Harvard_Board_of_Overseers "Harvard Board of Overseers"), [President and Fellows of Harvard College](/wiki/President_and_Fellows_of_Harvard_College "President and Fellows of Harvard College"), and [President of Harvard University](/wiki/President_of_Harvard_University "President of Harvard University")

Harvard is governed by a combination of its [Board of Overseers](/wiki/Harvard_Board_of_Overseers "Harvard Board of Overseers") and the [President and Fellows of Harvard College](/wiki/President_and_Fellows_of_Harvard_College "President and Fellows of Harvard College"), which is also known as the Harvard Corporation. These two bodies, in turn, appoint the [president of Harvard University](/wiki/President_of_Harvard_University "President of Harvard University").[[92]](#cite_note-93)

There are 16,000 staff and faculty,[[93]](#cite_note-94) including 2,400 professors, lecturers, and instructors in the colleges proper,[[94]](#cite_note-95) plus another >10,400 academic appointments in affiliated teaching hospitals)[[95]](#cite_note-96)

As of 2025, Harvard differs radically from its peer universities in two important ways. First, Harvard does not make its governing statutes publicly available, meaning that members of the Harvard community interested in reform must first persuade the university to give them a copy of those documents. Second, Harvard does not have an [academic senate](/wiki/Academic_senate "Academic senate") like most of its peers, although it is currently attempting to create one.[[96]](#cite_note-Heller-97)

### Endowment

Main article: [Harvard University endowment](/wiki/Harvard_University_endowment "Harvard University endowment")

Harvard has the largest [university endowment](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_the_United_States_by_endowment "List of colleges and universities in the United States by endowment") in the world, valued at about $50.7 billion as of 2023.[[19]](#cite_note-BGendow-19)[[20]](#cite_note-HFRendow-20)

During the [recession of 2007–2009](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), it suffered significant losses that forced large budget cuts, in particular temporarily halting construction on the Allston Science Complex.[[97]](#cite_note-98) The endowment has since recovered.[[98]](#cite_note-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102)

About $2 billion of investment income is annually distributed to fund operations.[[102]](#cite_note-103)
Harvard's ability to fund its degree and financial aid programs depends on the performance of its endowment; a poor performance in fiscal year 2016 forced a 4.4% cut in the number of graduate students funded by the [Faculty of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Harvard Faculty of Arts and Sciences").[[103]](#cite_note-104)
Endowment income is critical, as only 22% of revenue is from students' tuition, fees, room, and board.[[104]](#cite_note-105)

#### Divestment

Since the 1970s, several student-led campaigns have advocated [divesting](/wiki/Divestment "Divestment") Harvard's endowment from controversial holdings, including investments in [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") during [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan") during the [Darfur genocide](/wiki/Darfur_genocide_(2003%E2%80%932005) "Darfur genocide (2003–2005)"), and [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco_industry "Tobacco industry"), [fossil fuel](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel"), and [private prison](/wiki/Private_prison "Private prison") industries.[[105]](#cite_note-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107)

In the late 1980s, during the [disinvestment from South Africa](/wiki/Disinvestment_from_South_Africa "Disinvestment from South Africa") movement, student activists erected a symbolic [shanty town](/wiki/Shanty_town "Shanty town") on Harvard Yard and blockaded a speech by South African Vice Consul Duke Kent-Brown.[[107]](#cite_note-GeorgeKaufman2012-108)[[108]](#cite_note-109)

In response to pressure, the university eventually reduced its South African holdings by $230 million out of a total of $400 million between 1986 and 1987.[[107]](#cite_note-GeorgeKaufman2012-108)[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### Harvard College

Main article: [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College")

[Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College"), the four-year, full-time undergraduate program, has a [liberal arts and sciences](/wiki/Liberal_arts_education "Liberal arts education") focus.[[113]](#cite_note-Carnegie-114)[[114]](#cite_note-liberal-115) To graduate in the usual four years, undergraduates normally take 16 credit hours (usually across four courses) per semester for a total of 128 credit hours.[[118]](#cite_note-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120)
In most majors, an honors degree requires advanced coursework and a senior thesis.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

Though some introductory courses have large enrollments, the median class size is 12 students.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

### Harvard Medical School

Main article: [Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Harvard_Medical_School_%2854954429258%29.jpg/250px-Harvard_Medical_School_%2854954429258%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvard_Medical_School_(54954429258).jpg)

[Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School")

[Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School"), the third-oldest medical school in the United States, was founded in 1782 as Massachusetts Medical College by [John Warren](/wiki/John_Warren_(surgeon,_born_1753) "John Warren (surgeon, born 1753)"), [Benjamin Waterhouse](/wiki/Benjamin_Waterhouse "Benjamin Waterhouse"), and Aaron Dexter. In 1810, Harvard Medical School relocated from Cambridge to [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"). The medical school was tied to the rest of the university "only by the tenuous thread of degrees", but its strong faculty gave it a national reputation by the early 19th century.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

The medical school moved to its current location on Longwood Avenue in 1906, where the "Great White Quadrangle" or HMS Quad with its five white marble buildings was established.[[123]](#cite_note-124)[[124]](#cite_note-125)

Harvard Medical School's reputation continued to grow into the 20th century, especially in terms of scientific research and support from regional and national elites. Fifteen scientists won the [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") for work done at the Medical School.[[125]](#cite_note-126)[[126]](#cite_note-127) Its four major flagship teaching hospitals are [Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center](/wiki/Beth_Israel_Deaconess_Medical_Center "Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center"), [Brigham and Women's Hospital](/wiki/Brigham_and_Women%27s_Hospital "Brigham and Women's Hospital"), [Boston Children's Hospital](/wiki/Boston_Children%27s_Hospital "Boston Children's Hospital") and [Massachusetts General Hospital](/wiki/Massachusetts_General_Hospital "Massachusetts General Hospital").[[127]](#cite_note-128)

### Harvard Law School

Main article: [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/47/Langdell.jpg)](/wiki/File:Langdell.jpg)

[Christopher Columbus Langdell](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus_Langdell "Christopher Columbus Langdell"), an influential dean of [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School") from 1875 to 1890

The establishment of Harvard Law School in 1817 was made possible by a 1779 bequest from [Isaac Royall Jr.](/wiki/Isaac_Royall_Jr. "Isaac Royall Jr."); it is the oldest continuously operating law school in the nation.[[128]](#cite_note-Gershman-129) It was a small operation and grew slowly. By 1827, it was down to one faculty member. [Nathan Dane](/wiki/Nathan_Dane "Nathan Dane"), a prominent alumnus, endowed the Dane Professorship of Law and insisted that it be given to then Supreme Court Justice [Joseph Story](/wiki/Joseph_Story "Joseph Story"). For a while, the school was called Dane Law School.[[129]](#cite_note-130) Story's belief in the need for an elite law school based on merit and dedicated to public service helped build the school's reputation at the time. Enrollment remained low as academic legal education was considered to be of little added benefit to apprenticeships in legal practice.

Radical reform came in the 1870s, under Dean [Christopher Columbus Langdell](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus_Langdell "Christopher Columbus Langdell") (1826–1906).[[130]](#cite_note-131) Its new curriculum set the national standard and was copied widely in the United States. Langdell developed the [case method](/wiki/Casebook_method "Casebook method") of teaching law, based on his belief that law could be studied as a "science" gave university legal education a reason for being distinct from vocational preparation. The school introduced a first-year curriculum that was widely imitated, based on classes in [contracts](/wiki/Contracts "Contracts"), [property](/wiki/Property "Property"), [torts](/wiki/Torts "Torts"), [criminal law](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law") and [civil procedure](/wiki/Civil_procedure "Civil procedure").

Critics bemoaned abandonment of the more traditional lecture method, because of its efficiency and the lower workloads it placed on faculty and students. Advocates of the case method had a sounder theoretical basis in scientific research and the inductive method. Langdell's graduates became leading professors at other law schools where they introduced the case method. From its founding in 1900, the Association of American Law Schools promoted the case method in law schools that sought accreditation.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

### Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Science

Main article: [Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Arts_and_Sciences "Harvard Graduate School of Arts and Sciences")

As the college modernized in the late 19th century, the faculty was organized into departments and began to add graduate programs, including the PhD. [Charles William Eliot](/wiki/Charles_William_Eliot "Charles William Eliot"), president from 1869 to 1909, was a chemist who had spent two years in Germany studying at their universities. Thousands of Americans, mostly Harvard and Yale alumni, had attended German universities, especially Berlin and Göttingen.[[132]](#cite_note-133) Eliot used the German model to set up graduate programs at Harvard and he formed a graduate department in 1872, which granted its first Ph.D. degrees in 1873 to [William Byerly](/wiki/William_Elwood_Byerly "William Elwood Byerly") in mathematics and Charles Whitney in history. Eliot set up the Graduate School of Arts and Sciences with its own dean and budget in 1890, which dealt with graduate students and funded research programs.[[133]](#cite_note-134)

By 2004, there were 3,200 graduate students in 53 separate programs and forty former or current professors had won a Nobel Prize, most of them scientists or economists based in the Graduate School of Arts and Sciences.[[134]](#cite_note-135)

### Harvard Business School

Main article: [Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Aerial_of_the_Harvard_Business_School_campus.jpeg/250px-Aerial_of_the_Harvard_Business_School_campus.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_of_the_Harvard_Business_School_campus.jpeg)

Aerial view of [Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School")

From its founding in 1908, [Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School") has had a close relationship with the corporate world. Within a few years of its founding, many business leaders were its alumni and were hiring other alumni for starting positions in their firms.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-138) The school used Rockefeller funding in the 1920s to launch a major research program under [Elton Mayo](/wiki/Elton_Mayo "Elton Mayo") (1926–1947) for his "Harvard human relations group". Its findings revolutionized human relations in business and raised the reputation of the Business School from its initial "low status as a trainer of money grabbers into a high prestige educator of socially-conscientious administrators".[[138]](#cite_note-139) Starting in 1935, the school began weekend and short-term leadership training workshops for executives of major corporations that further expanded its national role.[[139]](#cite_note-140)

By 1949, nearly half of all the holders of the [MBA](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration") degree in the U.S. were alumni of Harvard Business School, and it was considered "the most influential graduate school of business".[[140]](#cite_note-141)

### Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health

Main article: [Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health](/wiki/Harvard_T.H._Chan_School_of_Public_Health "Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health")

The Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health is the public health school of Harvard University, located in the [Longwood Medical Area](/wiki/Longwood_Medical_and_Academic_Area "Longwood Medical and Academic Area") of Boston. The school grew out of the Harvard-MIT School for Health Officers,[[141]](#cite_note-142)[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144)[[144]](#cite_note-145)[[145]](#cite_note-146) the nation's first graduate training program in [population health](/wiki/Population_health "Population health"), which was founded in 1913 and then became the Harvard School of Public Health in 1922.

The school was part of Harvard Medical School until 1946, when it became a fully autonomous institution with its own dedicated public health and medical faculty.[[146]](#cite_note-147) It was renamed the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health in 2014 in honor of a $350 million donation from the Morningside Foundation.[[147]](#cite_note-148)

### Harvard Kennedy School

Main article: [Harvard Kennedy School](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Harvard_Kennedy_School_Littauer_Building.jpg/250px-Harvard_Kennedy_School_Littauer_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harvard_Kennedy_School_Littauer_Building.jpg)

The Littauer Building at [Harvard Kennedy School](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School")

In 1936, Harvard University founded the Harvard Graduate School of Public Administration, later renamed [Harvard Kennedy School](/wiki/Harvard_Kennedy_School "Harvard Kennedy School") in honor of former [U.S. President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") and 1940 [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College") alumnus [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy").
The Kennedy School has an endowment of $1.7 billion as of 2021 and is routinely ranked at the top of the world's graduate schools in public policy, social policy, international affairs, and government.[[148]](#cite_note-grad-schools.usnews.rankingsandreviews.com-149)[[149]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-150)[[150]](#cite_note-ivorytower2012-151)[[151]](#cite_note-tripsurvey-152)
Its alumni include 17 [heads of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") or [government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[148]](#cite_note-grad-schools.usnews.rankingsandreviews.com-149)[[149]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-150)[[150]](#cite_note-ivorytower2012-151)[[151]](#cite_note-tripsurvey-152)

### Research

Harvard is a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities")[[152]](#cite_note-153) and a preeminent research university with "very high" research activity (R1) and comprehensive doctoral programs across the arts, sciences, engineering, and medicine, according to the [Carnegie Classification](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education").[[113]](#cite_note-Carnegie-114)

The [medical school](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School") consistently ranks first among medical schools for research,[[153]](#cite_note-154) and biomedical research is an area of particular strength for the university. More than 11,000 faculty and 1,600 graduate students conduct research at the medical school and its 15 affiliated hospitals and research institutes.[[154]](#cite_note-155) In 2019, the medical school and its affiliates attracted $1.65 billion in competitive research grants from the [National Institutes of Health](/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health"), more than twice that of any other university.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

### Libraries

Main article: [Harvard Library](/wiki/Harvard_Library "Harvard Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Widener_Library.jpg/250px-Widener_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:Widener_Library.jpg)

[Widener Library](/wiki/Widener_Library "Widener Library"), the anchor of [Harvard Library](/wiki/Harvard_Library "Harvard Library"), the largest [academic library](/wiki/Academic_library "Academic library") in the world with more than 20 million holdings

[Harvard Library](/wiki/Harvard_Library "Harvard Library"), the largest [academic library](/wiki/Academic_library "Academic library") in the world with 20.4 million holdings, is centered in [Widener Library](/wiki/Widener_Library "Widener Library") in [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard"). It includes 25 individual Harvard libraries around the world with a combined staff of more than 800 librarians and personnel.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

[Houghton Library](/wiki/Houghton_Library "Houghton Library"), the Arthur and Elizabeth Schlesinger Library on the History of Women in America, and the Harvard University Archives consist principally of rare and unique materials. The nation's oldest collection of maps, gazetteers, and atlases is stored in [Pusey Library](/wiki/Pusey_Library "Pusey Library") on [Harvard Yard](/wiki/Harvard_Yard "Harvard Yard"), which is open to the public. The largest collection of [East-Asian](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") language material outside of East Asia is held in [Harvard-Yenching Library](/wiki/Harvard-Yenching_Library "Harvard-Yenching Library").

Other major libraries in the Harvard Library system include [Baker Library/Bloomberg Center](/wiki/Baker_Library/Bloomberg_Center "Baker Library/Bloomberg Center") at [Harvard Business School](/wiki/Harvard_Business_School "Harvard Business School"), [Cabot Science Library](/wiki/Cabot_Science_Library "Cabot Science Library") at [Harvard Science Center](/wiki/Harvard_Science_Center "Harvard Science Center"), [Dumbarton Oaks](/wiki/Dumbarton_Oaks "Dumbarton Oaks") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [Gutman Library](/wiki/Monroe_C._Gutman_Library "Monroe C. Gutman Library") at the [Harvard Graduate School of Education](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_School_of_Education "Harvard Graduate School of Education"), [Harvard Film Archive](/wiki/Harvard_Film_Archive "Harvard Film Archive") at the [Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts](/wiki/Carpenter_Center_for_the_Visual_Arts "Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts"), [Houghton Library](/wiki/Houghton_Library "Houghton Library"), and [Lamont Library](/wiki/Lamont_Library "Lamont Library").

### Museums

Main article: [Harvard Art Museums](/wiki/Harvard_Art_Museums "Harvard Art Museums")

[Harvard Art Museums](/wiki/Harvard_Art_Museums "Harvard Art Museums") includes three museums, the [Arthur M. Sackler Museum](/wiki/Arthur_M._Sackler_Museum "Arthur M. Sackler Museum") covers Asian, Mediterranean, and Islamic art; the [Busch–Reisinger Museum](/wiki/Busch%E2%80%93Reisinger_Museum "Busch–Reisinger Museum") (formerly the Germanic Museum) covers central and northern European art; and the [Fogg Museum](/wiki/Fogg_Museum "Fogg Museum") covers Western art from the Middle Ages to the present emphasizing Italian [early Renaissance](/wiki/Early_Renaissance_painting "Early Renaissance painting"), British [pre-Raphaelite](/wiki/Pre-Raphaelite "Pre-Raphaelite"), and 19th-century French art.

Harvard Museums of Science and Culture include the [Harvard Museum of Natural History](/wiki/Harvard_Museum_of_Natural_History "Harvard Museum of Natural History"), which itself includes the [Harvard Mineralogical and Geological Museum](/wiki/Harvard_Mineralogical_Museum "Harvard Mineralogical Museum"), the [Harvard University Herbaria](/wiki/Harvard_University_Herbaria "Harvard University Herbaria") featuring the [Blaschka](/wiki/Leopold_and_Rudolf_Blaschka "Leopold and Rudolf Blaschka") [Glass Flowers](/wiki/Glass_Flowers "Glass Flowers") exhibit, and the [Museum of Comparative Zoology](/wiki/Museum_of_Comparative_Zoology "Museum of Comparative Zoology"). Others include the [Harvard Collection of Historical Scientific Instruments](/wiki/Harvard_Collection_of_Historical_Scientific_Instruments "Harvard Collection of Historical Scientific Instruments") at [Harvard Science Center](/wiki/Harvard_Science_Center "Harvard Science Center"), the [Harvard Museum of the Ancient Near East](/wiki/Harvard_Museum_of_the_Ancient_Near_East "Harvard Museum of the Ancient Near East") featuring artifacts from excavations in the Middle East, and the [Peabody Museum of Archaeology and Ethnology](/wiki/Peabody_Museum_of_Archaeology_and_Ethnology "Peabody Museum of Archaeology and Ethnology"), specializing in the cultural history and civilizations of the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"), the [Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts](/wiki/Carpenter_Center_for_the_Visual_Arts "Carpenter Center for the Visual Arts"), designed by [Le Corbusier](/wiki/Le_Corbusier "Le Corbusier") and housing the [Harvard Film Archive](/wiki/Harvard_Film_Archive "Harvard Film Archive"), the [Warren Anatomical Museum](/wiki/Warren_Anatomical_Museum "Warren Anatomical Museum") at [Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School")'s [Center for the History of Medicine](/wiki/Center_for_the_History_of_Medicine "Center for the History of Medicine"), and the Ethelbert Cooper Gallery of African & African American Art at the [Hutchins Center for African and African American Research](/wiki/Hutchins_Center_for_African_and_African_American_Research "Hutchins Center for African and African American Research").

Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences") was ranked the third-best school in the world for engineering and technology by *[Times Higher Education](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education "Times Higher Education")*.[[173]](#cite_note-174)

In [international relations](/wiki/International_relations "International relations"), *[Foreign Policy](/wiki/Foreign_Policy "Foreign Policy")* magazine ranks Harvard best in the world at the undergraduate level and second in the world at the graduate level, behind the [Walsh School of Foreign Service](/wiki/Walsh_School_of_Foreign_Service "Walsh School of Foreign Service") at [Georgetown University](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University").[[174]](#cite_note-175)

## Student activities

### Student government

Further information: [Harvard Graduate Council](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_Council "Harvard Graduate Council")

The [Undergraduate Council](/wiki/Harvard_Undergraduate_Council "Harvard Undergraduate Council") represented Harvard College undergraduate students until it was dissolved in 2022,[[176]](#cite_note-179) and replaced by the Undergraduate Association. The [Graduate Council](/wiki/Harvard_Graduate_Council "Harvard Graduate Council") represents students at all twelve graduate and professional schools, most of which also have their own student government.[[177]](#cite_note-180)

### Student media

Further information: [The Harvard Crimson](/wiki/The_Harvard_Crimson "The Harvard Crimson")

*[The Harvard Crimson](/wiki/The_Harvard_Crimson "The Harvard Crimson")*, founded in 1873 and run entirely by Harvard undergraduate students, is the university's primary [student newspaper](/wiki/Student_publication "Student publication"). Many [notable alumni](/wiki/List_of_The_Harvard_Crimson_people "List of The Harvard Crimson people") have worked at the *Crimson*, including two [U.S. presidents](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") (AB, 1903) and [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") (AB 1940).

## Athletics

Main article: [Harvard Crimson](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson "Harvard Crimson")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Cornell_vs._Harvard_football_Oct_12%2C_2019.jpg/250px-Cornell_vs._Harvard_football_Oct_12%2C_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cornell_vs._Harvard_football_Oct_12,_2019.jpg)

[Harvard football](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_football "Harvard Crimson football") (right) taking on [Cornell](/wiki/Cornell_Big_Red_football "Cornell Big Red football") (left) at [Harvard Stadium](/wiki/Harvard_Stadium "Harvard Stadium") in October 2019

Harvard College competes in the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") [Division I](/wiki/Division_I_(NCAA) "Division I (NCAA)") [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") conference. The school fields 42 intercollegiate sports teams, more than any other college in the country.[[178]](#cite_note-181)

Harvard and the other seven [Ivy League](/wiki/Ivy_League "Ivy League") universities are prohibited from offering [athletic scholarships](/wiki/Athletic_scholarship "Athletic scholarship").[[179]](#cite_note-182) The school color is [crimson](/wiki/Crimson "Crimson").[[180]](#cite_note-183)

### National championships

In the NCAA Division I era, which began in 1973, Harvard Crimson teams have won five NCAA Division I championships as of 2024: [men's ice hockey](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_men%27s_ice_hockey "Harvard Crimson men's ice hockey") in 1989, women's lacrosse in 1990, women's rowing in 2003, and men's fencing in 2006 and 2024. Including the pre-NCAA era, Harvard has won 159 national championships across all sports. Its [men's squash team](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_men%27s_squash "Harvard Crimson men's squash") holds the record for the most national collegiate championships in the sport. Harvard's first national championship came in 1880, when its track and field team won the national championship.[[181]](#cite_note-184)

### Rivalries

Further information: [Cornell–Harvard hockey rivalry](/wiki/Cornell%E2%80%93Harvard_hockey_rivalry "Cornell–Harvard hockey rivalry") and [Harvard–Yale football rivalry](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry")

Harvard's athletic programs maintain a long-standing rivalry with [Yale](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs "Yale Bulldogs") in all sports, especially in [college football](/wiki/College_football "College football"), where [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_football "Harvard Crimson football") and [Yale](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs_football "Yale Bulldogs football") compete in an [annual football rivalry](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry"), which has played 139 times as of 2024, dating back to its first meeting in 1875.[[182]](#cite_note-185)

Every two years, Harvard and Yale track and field teams come together to compete against a combined [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") team in the oldest continuous international amateur competition in the world.[[183]](#cite_note-186)

In [men's ice hockey](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_men%27s_ice_hockey "Harvard Crimson men's ice hockey"), Harvard maintains a [historic rivalry](/wiki/Cornell%E2%80%93Harvard_hockey_rivalry "Cornell–Harvard hockey rivalry") with [Cornell](/wiki/Cornell_Big_Red_men%27s_ice_hockey "Cornell Big Red men's ice hockey"), which dates back to their first meeting in 1910. The two teams play twice annually.

In [men's rugby](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), Harvard maintains a rivalry with [McGill](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University"), as demonstrated by the biennial Harvard-McGill rugby games, alternately played in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and Cambridge.[[184]](#cite_note-187)

## Notable people

### Alumni

Further information: [List of Harvard University people](/wiki/List_of_Harvard_University_people "List of Harvard University people"), [List of Harvard University non-graduate alumni](/wiki/List_of_Harvard_University_non-graduate_alumni "List of Harvard University non-graduate alumni"), and [List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_university_affiliation "List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation")

Since its founding nearly four centuries ago, Harvard alumni have distinguished themselves in academia, activism, arts, athletics, business, entrepreneurship, government, international affairs, journalism, media, music, non-profit organizations, politics, public policy, science, technology, writing, and other industries and fields. A 2024 study analyzed the educational backgrounds of the most successful and influential Americans—"30 different achievement groups totaling 26,198 people"—and found that Harvard alumni were unusually dominant.[[185]](#cite_note-Wai-188) A 2025 study of 6,141 of the most influential people in the world discovered that Harvard alumni are massively overrepresented among the global elite, and that this finding remains true when all American elites are removed.[[186]](#cite_note-Salas-Díaz-189)

Among the world's universities and colleges, Harvard has the most [U.S. presidents](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_United_States_by_education "List of presidents of the United States by education") (eight), [living billionaires](/wiki/List_of_universities_by_number_of_billionaire_alumni "List of universities by number of billionaire alumni") (188), [Nobel](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_university_affiliation "List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation") laureates (162), [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners (48), [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal") recipients (seven), [Marshall scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship") (252), and [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship") (369) among its alumni. Harvard alumni also include nine [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") laureates, ten [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") winners, and [108 Olympic medalists](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medalist_students_and_alumni "List of American universities with Olympic medalist students and alumni"), including 46 gold medal winners.[[187]](#cite_note-190)[[188]](#cite_note-191)[[189]](#cite_note-192)[[190]](#cite_note-193)[[191]](#cite_note-194)[[192]](#cite_note-195)

## In popular culture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Clock_Tower_University_of_Puerto_Rico-San_Marcos-Harvard.jpg/250px-Clock_Tower_University_of_Puerto_Rico-San_Marcos-Harvard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clock_Tower_University_of_Puerto_Rico-San_Marcos-Harvard.jpg)

Tower at the [University of Puerto Rico](/wiki/University_of_Puerto_Rico "University of Puerto Rico"), showing the emblem of Harvard (on right), the oldest in the United States, and that of [National University of San Marcos](/wiki/National_University_of_San_Marcos "National University of San Marcos"), Lima (left), the oldest in the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas")

Harvard's reputation as a center of elite achievement or elitist privilege has made it a frequent literary and cinematic backdrop. "In the grammar of film, Harvard has come to mean both tradition, and a certain amount of stuffiness," film critic Paul Sherman said in 2010.[[207]](#cite_note-210)

### Literature

In contemporary literature, Harvard University features prominently in multiple novels, including:

* *[The Sound and the Fury](/wiki/The_Sound_and_the_Fury "The Sound and the Fury")* (1929) and *[Absalom, Absalom!](/wiki/Absalom,_Absalom! "Absalom, Absalom!")* (1936), two novels by [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner"), both of which depict Harvard student life.[[208]](#cite_note-211)
* *[Of Time and the River](/wiki/Of_Time_and_the_River "Of Time and the River")* (1935) by [Thomas Wolfe](/wiki/Thomas_Wolfe "Thomas Wolfe"), a fictionalized autobiography, depicting Wolfe's [alter ego](/wiki/Alter_ego "Alter ego"), Eugene Gant, a Harvard student.[[209]](#cite_note-Vaughan_Bail-1958-212)
* *[The Late George Apley](/wiki/The_Late_George_Apley "The Late George Apley")* (1937), by 1915 Harvard alumnus [John P. Marquand](/wiki/John_P._Marquand "John P. Marquand"), a novel presenting a satirical view of Harvard men in the early 20th century,[[209]](#cite_note-Vaughan_Bail-1958-212) which was awarded the [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction").[[210]](#cite_note-213)
* *The Second Happiest Day* (1953), by John P. Marquand, portrays Harvard during the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") generation.[[211]](#cite_note-214)[[212]](#cite_note-215)[[213]](#cite_note-216)[[214]](#cite_note-217)[[215]](#cite_note-218)

### Films

Harvard University features prominently in the plots of multiple major films, including:

* *[Love Story](/wiki/Love_Story_(1970_film) "Love Story (1970 film)")* (1970), a romance between a wealthy [Harvard ice hockey](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_men%27s_ice_hockey "Harvard Crimson men's ice hockey") player, played by [Ryan O'Neal](/wiki/Ryan_O%27Neal "Ryan O'Neal"), and a brilliant Radcliffe student of modest means, played by [Ali MacGraw](/wiki/Ali_MacGraw "Ali MacGraw").[[216]](#cite_note-219)[[217]](#cite_note-220)[[218]](#cite_note-221)
* *[The Paper Chase](/wiki/The_Paper_Chase_(film) "The Paper Chase (film)")* (1973),[[219]](#cite_note-222) a drama based on the 1971 [novel of the same name](/wiki/The_Paper_Chase_(Osborn_novel) "The Paper Chase (Osborn novel)") by Harvard alumnus [John Jay Osborn Jr.](/wiki/John_Jay_Osborn_Jr. "John Jay Osborn Jr."), about a first year [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School") student facing a demanding [contract law](/wiki/Contract_law "Contract law") course and professor.
* *[A Small Circle of Friends](/wiki/A_Small_Circle_of_Friends "A Small Circle of Friends")* (1980), a drama about three Harvard University students in the 1960s
* *[Prozac Nation](/wiki/Prozac_Nation_(film) "Prozac Nation (film)")* (1994), a psychological drama starring [Christina Ricci](/wiki/Christina_Ricci "Christina Ricci") based on the [novel of the same name](/wiki/Prozac_Nation "Prozac Nation") by [Elizabeth Wurtzel](/wiki/Elizabeth_Wurtzel "Elizabeth Wurtzel"), which documents her real life story as a 19-year-old Harvard freshman struggling with [substance abuse](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse") and [clinical depression](/wiki/Major_depressive_disorder "Major depressive disorder").
* *[Legally Blonde](/wiki/Legally_Blonde "Legally Blonde")* (2001), a comedy film starring [Reese Witherspoon](/wiki/Reese_Witherspoon "Reese Witherspoon") a blonde [sorority](/wiki/Sorority "Sorority") girl who enrolls in [Harvard Law School](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School") to get her ex-boyfriend back.[[220]](#cite_note-223)
* *[Homeless to Harvard: The Liz Murray Story](/wiki/Homeless_to_Harvard:_The_Liz_Murray_Story "Homeless to Harvard: The Liz Murray Story")* (2003), a [Lifetime](/wiki/Lifetime_(TV_channel) "Lifetime (TV channel)") biographical [television film](/wiki/Television_film "Television film"), which chronicles the real life story of [Liz Murray](/wiki/Liz_Murray "Liz Murray") (played by [Thora Birch](/wiki/Thora_Birch "Thora Birch")), who overcomes homelessness and a dysfunctional family to gain entry and a scholarship to Harvard after winning a *[New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*-sponsored essay competition.[[221]](#cite_note-224)[[222]](#cite_note-225)
* *[The Social Network](/wiki/The_Social_Network "The Social Network")* (2010), a biographical drama film which portrays the founding of social networking website [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook").[[223]](#cite_note-226)

Crosby, Nichols, Lee & Company. p. 586. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-405-10016-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-405-10016-1 "Special:BookSources/978-0-405-10016-1"). Archived from [the original](https://books.google.com/books?id=KynqxH_4lGUC&pg=PA586) on September 6, 2015. `{{cite book}}`: ISBN / Date incompatibility ([help](/wiki/Help:CS1_errors#invalid_isbn_date "Help:CS1 errors")), "At a Court holden September 8th, 1636 and continued by adjournment to the 28th of the 8th month (October, 1636)... the Court agreed to give £400 towards a School or College, whereof £200 to be paid next year...." Tercentenary dates: ["Cambridge Birthday"](https://web.archive.org/web/20260222043339/https://time.com/archive/6755981/education-cambridge-birthday/). *Time*. September 28, 1936. Archived from [the original](https://time.com/archive/6755981/education-cambridge-birthday) on February 22, 2026. Retrieved September 8, 2006.: "Harvard claims birth on the day the Massachusetts Great and General Court convened to authorize its founding. This was Sept. 8, 1637 under the Julian calendar. Allowing for the ten-day advance of the Gregorian calendar, Tercentenary officials arrived at Sept. 18 as the date for the third and last big Day of the celebration;" "on Oct. 28, 1636 ... £400 for that 'school or college' [was voted by] the Great and General Court of the [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony")." Bicentennial date: Marvin Hightower (September 2, 2003). ["Harvard Gazette: This Month in Harvard History"](https://web.archive.org/web/20060908144409/http://www.news.harvard.edu/gazette/2003/10.02/02-history.html). Harvard University. Archived from [the original](http://www.news.harvard.edu/gazette/2003/10.02/02-history.html) on September 8, 2006. Retrieved September 15, 2006., "Sept. 8, 1836 – Some 1,100 to 1,300 alumni flock to Harvard's Bicentennial, at which a professional choir premieres "Fair Harvard." ... guest speaker Josiah Quincy Jr., Class of 1821, makes a motion, unanimously adopted, 'that this assembly of the Alumni be adjourned to meet at this place on September 8, 1936.'" Tercentary opening of Quincy's sealed package: *The New York Times*, September 9, 1936, p. 24, "Package Sealed in 1836 Opened at Harvard. It Held Letters Written at Bicentenary": "September 8th, 1936: As the first formal function in the celebration of Harvard's tercentenary, the Harvard Alumni Association witnessed the opening by President Conant of the 'mysterious' package sealed by President Josiah Quincy at the Harvard bicentennial in 1836."
